coredata.dc:description: This study explores how synthesis temperature influences the properties and functionalities of sulfur‑nitrogen doped carbon dots (S,N-doped CDs) derived from citric acid and cysteine. The focus lies on their potential in bionanocomposite films for active packaging. Bionanocomposite films were prepared by incorporating CDs synthesized at different temperatures (160, 180, and 200 °C) into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVOH) matrix. CD160 displayed a crystalline structure below 10 nm and demonstrated the strongest antifungal and antibacterial activity, as evidenced by the largest clear zones. This superior performance is presumably due to their high N and S content, as shown by the larger redshift in their photoluminescence spectra upon longer wavelength excitation and elemental analysis. CD180 lacked well-defined lattice fringes, indicating an amorphous structure with larger cluster sizes ranging from 10 to 100 nm. Interestingly, UV-blocking and antibacterial efficacy of both films remained high (over 95% UVA block and 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in bacteria) regardless of CD crystallinity and size. However, the PVOH-CD180 film exhibited more photoluminescence, most likely due to larger defect-related emission centers. In contrast, CD200 had the weakest antifungal and antibacterial activity. While the PVOH-CD200 film achieved a 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-positive bacteria, it only managed a 4 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-negative bacteria under contact conditions at 37 °C for 24 h. In addition, the PVOH-CD200 film had the highest antioxidant activity, 88% DPPH radical scavenging, whereas the others had 80%. These findings underscore the critical role of synthesis temperature in tailoring S,N-doped CDs for specific applications within active packaging, where the desired properties dictate the optimal temperature. Preliminary studies suggest that PVOH-CD films have the potential to extend the shelf-life of sweet bread, demonstrating their promise as bionanocomposite active food packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Influence of synthesis temperature on the active performance of doped‑carbon dots embedded in polyvinyl alcohol and their potential for active food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores how synthesis temperature influences the properties and functionalities of sulfur‑nitrogen doped carbon dots (S,N-doped CDs) derived from citric acid and cysteine. The focus lies on their potential in bionanocomposite films for active packaging. Bionanocomposite films were prepared by incorporating CDs synthesized at different temperatures (160, 180, and 200 °C) into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVOH) matrix. CD160 displayed a crystalline structure below 10 nm and demonstrated the strongest antifungal and antibacterial activity, as evidenced by the largest clear zones. This superior performance is presumably due to their high N and S content, as shown by the larger redshift in their photoluminescence spectra upon longer wavelength excitation and elemental analysis. CD180 lacked well-defined lattice fringes, indicating an amorphous structure with larger cluster sizes ranging from 10 to 100 nm. Interestingly, UV-blocking and antibacterial efficacy of both films remained high (over 95% UVA block and 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in bacteria) regardless of CD crystallinity and size. However, the PVOH-CD180 film exhibited more photoluminescence, most likely due to larger defect-related emission centers. In contrast, CD200 had the weakest antifungal and antibacterial activity. While the PVOH-CD200 film achieved a 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-positive bacteria, it only managed a 4 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-negative bacteria under contact conditions at 37 °C for 24 h. In addition, the PVOH-CD200 film had the highest antioxidant activity, 88% DPPH radical scavenging, whereas the others had 80%. These findings underscore the critical role of synthesis temperature in tailoring S,N-doped CDs for specific applications within active packaging, where the desired properties dictate the optimal temperature. Preliminary studies suggest that PVOH-CD films have the potential to extend the shelf-life of sweet bread, demonstrating their promise as bionanocomposite active food packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage frequently occurs due to the undesirable proliferation of bacteria and fungi. These minuscule organisms consume the food, leading to its deterioration and making it dangerous or unappetizing to consume. This has a significant effect on the shelf-life of a product, which refers to the duration of time it remains fresh. To address this challenge, there has been a surge in the development of active packaging technologies. These innovative packaging systems incorporate functional elements that can interact with the food and its surrounding environment. The interaction aims to control or extend the product's shelf-life through various mechanisms (Deshmukh & Gaikwad, 2024; Vermeiren et al., 1999). Over the past decade, a substantial amount of research has been dedicated to exploring and identifying active compounds with the potential to deliver functionalities such as UV-light blocking, antioxidation, antibacterial activity, and antifungal properties (Firouz et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2021). The development of multifunctional active compounds is particularly attractive and highly sought-after for various food applications due to their enhanced efficacy in food preservation. The endeavors have discovered prospective candidates including organic compounds (Lee et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022), extracted essential oils (Orsuwan & Sothornvit, 2018; Wang, Yang, et al., 2024), and nanomaterials (Purohit et al., 2023; Sani et al., 2023).

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials that have zero dimensionality and are approximately spherical in shape. They have attracted significant interest due to their distinct combination of characteristics, such as easy synthesis and modification, small size, strong fluorescence, low toxicity, and excellent ability to disperse in water (Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023; Li et al., 2024; Mathew et al., 2024; Yao et al., 2019). These characteristics make them promising candidates for integration into smart packaging applications. Recent progress in the synthesis of CDs from various carbon-based materials and the ability to precisely manipulate their size, surface properties, and doping have provided opportunities to customize their characteristics for specific purposes in active packaging. These include features like UV-blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial capabilities (Ghorbani et al., 2024; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023; Wang, Wang, et al., 2024). The continuous investigation of innovative technologies for synthesizing CDs seeks to fully exploit their capabilities and utilize their distinctive characteristics for advanced packaging solutions. This holds significant promise for the future of packaging technology, offering a wide array of exciting possibilities.

Hydrothermal synthesis has become the primary method for producing CDs, allowing for fine control of numerous synthesis parameters to achieve outstanding adjustability of their properties. Key factors such as temperature, reaction duration, pH, precursor ratio, and surface doping have a substantial impact on the properties of CDs (Kurian & Paul, 2021). The temperature is a critical element in the endothermic carbonization process and has a significant impact on the characteristics of CDs (Khayal et al., 2021). Surface modification and doping techniques also augment favorable characteristics such as fluorescence intensity, biocompatibility, water solubility, and stability. The inclusion of heteroatoms, specifically nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S), allows for precise manipulation of the internal and surface electrical and chemical properties of CDs. The higher electronegativity of N compared to carbon enhances the positive charge of CDs, resulting in the disruption of bacterial membrane integrity and improved antibacterial effectiveness (Chen et al., 2023; Kumar & Gaikwad, 2023). In contrast, the addition of S-doping has been demonstrated to greatly enhance the intensity of fluorescence emission (Khayal et al., 2021). Despite extensive research and application of S,N-doped-CDs in catalytic systems (Anjana et al., 2018; Karaman, 2021; Kaushal et al., 2023), biomedical fields (Mohapatra et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2022), and antimicrobial purposes (Suner et al., 2021), their potential for active packaging remains largely unexplored.

This study investigates the effects of the synthesis temperature on the physicochemical properties and functionality of colloidal CDs. The CDs are produced using a green hydrothermal approach. The selection of citric acid as the precursor is based on the fact that temperatures ranging from 140 to 250 °C are frequently used for the production of CDs generated from this source (Vallan & Imahori, 2022). It is important to mention that all other synthesis parameters, such as reaction time and pressure, were held constant throughout the experiment. The functionality of the CDs was modified by using cysteine, an amino acid that naturally contains sulfur. A comprehensive study was carried out to determine the effects of different synthesis temperatures (160, 180, and 200 °C) on the size and morphology of the particles, UV–vis absorption spectrum, photoluminescence emission, antibacterial and antifungal characteristics of the colloidal CDs. Subsequently, a transparent bionanocomposite film was prepared by integrating colloidal CDs into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVOH) matrix. This film is designed to serve as a packaging material that possesses multifunctional active performance. The effectiveness of film was assessed through measurements of UV-light barrier properties, antioxidant capacity, and antimicrobial efficacy. Finally, the potential of this film to extend the shelf-life of sweet bread was investigated and elucidated.

The citric acid monohydrate (CAS:5949-29-1, 99.5 %) was acquired from KemAus (NSW, Australia) and was used as received without additional purification steps. L-Cysteine (CAS: 52–90-4, 99%) was procured from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). The reaction and analytical solutions were produced using deionized water. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVOH) powder (CAS: C072PVA, pH 5–7) was purchased from Bangkok Chemical Co., Ltd. (Bangkok, Thailand).

The method was modified from Suner et al. (2021). The synthesis of CDs was conducted using a hydrothermal approach. A mixture of 4.5 g citric acid and 2.0 g L-cysteine was dissolved in 20 mL water and transferred to 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclaves. The individual reactors were subjected to different temperatures (160, 180, and 200 °C) for 6 h in forced-convection heating chambers (Binder GmbH, Germany). After being cooled to ambient temperature, the produced mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 15,000 ×g for 10 min at 25 °C using a UniCen MR centrifuge (Herolab GmbH Laborgeräte, Germany). The supernatant containing the CD was subsequently passed through a 0.22 μm VertiClean™ PTFE syringe filter (Ligand Scientific Co., Ltd., Thailand). The resultant transparent and sepia solutions were preserved in airtight containers for subsequent examination. In order to evaluate the impact of doping, a solution of citric acid without L-cysteine was subjected to the identical method at a temperature of 200 °C. Samples were labeled based on their synthesis temperature and presence/absence of doping: citric (non-doped), citric-160, citric-180, and citric-200.

Five grams of PVOH powder were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water under constant stirring at 80 ± 2 °C. The solution was stirred until completely homogeneous. After cooling to room temperature, predetermined volumes (1 mL, 30 wt%) of the as-prepared CD suspension were added to separate aliquots (9 mL and 99 mL, respectively) of the PVOH solution. These mixtures were thoroughly stirred to ensure uniform dispersion of the CDs. The obtained suspensions were designated Dilution#1 (3 wt% CD loading) and Dilution#2 (0.3 wt% CD loading), respectively, based on subsequent solid content analysis.

It is noteworthy that Dilution#1 was employed for all film characterization analyses, while both Dilution#1 and Dilution#2 were used for the antimicrobial performance testing. The well-mixed suspensions were then carefully poured onto pre-cleaned polystyrene plates (14 cm diameter). The plates were placed in an incubator set at a temperature of 30 ± 2 °C for a period of 48 h for drying process. The transparent films obtained were categorized based on their composition: PVOH (film without CDs as a control), PVOH-CD160, PVOH-CD180, and PVOH-CD200, which correspond to the temperature at which the CDs were synthesized.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs were recorded using an Evolution 300 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescence analysis was conducted on a FluoroMax plus fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Scientific, USA) with excitation wavelengths of 340, 350, and 360 nm. Prior to analysis, the as-prepared CD suspensions were serially diluted tenfold with deionized water. Data collection was performed using samples from the 10−2 dilution.

The morphology and microstructure of the CDs were investigated by field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM) on a JEM-3100F microscope (JOEL, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. A 10−3 dilution of the CD suspension was drop-cast onto a carbon coated copper grids and allowed to dry for analysis. Elemental composition of the CDs was determined by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis.

The antibacterial activity of CD suspensions was assessed using the well diffusion method on agar plates. The extent of bacterial growth inhibition was measured as the diameter of the inhibition zone in millimeters (mm). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) ATCC 25923 and Escherichia coli (E. coli) ATCC 8739 were employed as reference strains for the antibacterial assay. Briefly, agar plates were inoculated with the respective bacterial strain under aseptic conditions. Subsequently, wells with a diameter of 5 mm were created in the agar and filled with 50 μL of the CD suspension. The inoculated plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, the diameter of the clear zones surrounding the wells, indicative of inhibited bacterial growth, were measured.

Antifungal activity of CDs against Penicillium spp. and Aspergillus niger was determined using the agar disk diffusion method. Fungal cultures were grown on potato dextrose agar (PDA) at 25 °C for 5 days. Spore suspensions were prepared in sterile distilled water and adjusted to a concentration of 100 spores/mL. PDA plates were inoculated with the fungal suspension and CD solutions (100 μL) were dropped on the agar surface. Plates were sealed with Parafilm® and incubated at 25 °C. Inhibition zones were measured on Day3 and Day5 using a Vernier caliper and calculated as the average diameter of the inhibition zones.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR Spectrometer (Bruker Optics Ltd., UK). Elemental composition of the bionanocomposite films for carbon (C), hydrogen (H), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) was determined by CHNS/O analyzer 628 series (Leco Corporation, USA).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical structure of the CDs. The analysis was performed using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical, UK) with a binding energy range of 0 to 1200 eV. The chamber was maintained at a base pressure of approximately 5 × 10−9 Torr through turbomolecular pumping. To mitigate water vapor interference, the PVOH-CD films underwent pre-vacuum treatment.

The wettability of the film surfaces was evaluated by measuring the water contact angle using a Data-Physics OCA 15 EC contact angle analyzer (Data Physics Instruments GmbH, Germany). The surface morphology of the film samples was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) performed on a FEI Quanta 450 instrument (FEI Company, USA).

The color properties of the prepared bionanocomposite films were evaluated using a HunterLab UltraScan VIS colorimeter (HunterLab, USA) to obtain their CIELab values. The photoluminescence emission characteristics of the as-prepared bionanocomposite films were captured using a digital camera under 365 nm UV light excitation against a black background. The optical properties of the bionanocomposite films, including their UV light absorption and transmittance, were investigated within the 200–800 nm wavelength range using a double-beam V-600 spectrophotometer (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan).

The antioxidant activity of the bionanocomposite films was evaluated using the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging assay. Briefly, samples were cut into small pieces (approximately 3.0 × 3.0 mm2) and 1.0 g of these pieces were immersed in 5.0 mL of a 0.1 mM DPPH solution prepared in absolute ethanol within a glass tube. The tubes were then incubated in a dark chamber for 60 mins. The radical scavenging activity was quantified by measuring the absorbance of the DPPH solution at 515 nm using UV–vis spectroscopy. The percentage of DPPH radical scavenging activity (% scavenging) was calculated using the following equation:
(1)

Antibacterial activity of the bionanocomposite films was evaluated according to ASTM E2149–01 against S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and E. coli (ATCC 8739) with minor modifications. Because the as-prepared bionanocomposite film was highly water soluble in vigorous dynamic condition, the shaking process was omitted from this study. Briefly, the final concentration of 1.5 × 105 CFU/mL was obtained by diluting with nutrient broth solution. One gram of each test sample was first sterilized with UV light and added to 10 mL bacteria solution in screw-cap conical tube. For viable cell enumeration, 1 mL of the solutions was immediately pipetted, placed in a test tube containing 9 mL of nutrient broth and mixed thoroughly. Then, 1 mL from this test tube was taken with a new pipette and placed in another test tube containing 9 mL of peptone. These procedures were repeated to prepare 10-fold serial dilutions. Subsequently, a 400 μL aliquot of each microbial suspension was dispensed onto an agar plate and incubated at 35 ± 1 °C. As soon as the zero-contact time sub-samples were prepared, other samples were kept for 24 h and the same procedure was repeated. Antimicrobial activity was evidenced by the reduction of microbial colonies. After 24 h incubation, 30 to 300 colony-forming units (CFUs) in a serially diluted petri dish were counted. Antibacterial activity was calculated as the percentage reduction of the colonies using eq. (2):
(2)

%
=
A − B
A
× 100
where A is the average number of viable cells for addition of film samples at zero-contact time and B is the average number of viable cells from the flask containing film samples after 24 h contact time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as active packaging for sweet bread
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh bread loaves were obtained from a local bakery in Bangkok, Thailand, on the same day they underwent quality assessment (Day0). The loaf was cut into slices measuring 5 cm by 7 cm and 1 cm in thickness. Experimental films (PVOH, PVOH-CD160, PVOH-CD180, PVOH-CD200) were cut into 8.5 × 8.5 cm squares and applied to the top and bottom surfaces of bread slices. The slices were then sealed in 15 × 20 cm polypropylene bags to prevent moisture loss. Control samples were packaged in polypropylene bags without film. Triplicate samples were stored at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C). Digital images of each bread sample under the various packaging conditions were captured on Day0, Day2, Day4, and Day6 to assess bread appearance.To evaluate the bread's hardness, the slice was taken out of its packaging and promptly analyzed using a Universal Testing Machine (Micro 350, Testometric Co., Ltd., UK). Analyzed samples utilizing a load cell with a maximum capacity of 50 N were done using a cylindrical probe with a diameter of 36 mm. The analysis was conducted at a speed of 1 mm/min. Six replicates were performed per sample condition to ensure statistical accuracy.

Water activity (Aw) of bread was measured using a Testo 650 Water Activity Meter (Testo Inc., USA). Bread samples were removed from packaging, cut into small pieces (approximately 3 cm diameter), and placed in a plastic cup for Aw determination.

The SPSS statistical program (SPSS 27.0 for Windows, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used for data analysis. Differences between factors and levels were evaluated by analysis of variance (ANOVA). Duncan's multiple range test was used to compare means to identify which groups were significantly different from other groups (p < 0.05).

According to prior research, citric acid begins to undergo thermal decomposition at a temperature of 180 °C. However, it has been found that lower temperatures can also yield citric acid derived CDs if the reaction period is extended (Vallan & Imahori, 2022). In order to examine the impact of synthesis temperature on the formation and properties of CDs, a transparent solution of citric acid mixed with cysteine was exposed to a hydrothermal procedure at different temperatures (160, 180, and 200 °C). After undergoing hydrothermal treatment, the pure citric acid solution retained a very faint brown color, which could only be noticed with careful inspection (Fig. 1a inset). In contrast, the samples containing cysteine showed a dark brown color, emphasizing the crucial role of cysteine in formation of CDs. Fig. 1a presents the absorbance spectra of the as-obtained suspensions from their corresponding 10−2 dilution, with 10-fold serial dilution shown in Fig. 1b-d. All CD suspensions displayed a prominent broad absorption peak ranging from 345 to 355 nm. This peak was absent in the spectrum of the undoped-citric acid solution. Interestingly, the peak position in the doped-CD suspensions exhibited a slight blue shift with increasing synthesis temperature, suggesting a temperature-dependent growth mechanism for the CDs (Ibrayev et al., 2022). The absorption characteristic around 350 nm originates from n−π* edge transitions, which are associated with the CD core and represent the primary emissive centers for their intense luminescence (Kumar et al., 2022; Suner et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2009). In this study, the n−π* transition could be attributed to the presence of surface carboxyl and amino groups resulting from cysteine doping (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023; Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, a shoulder peak was observed in the range of 235 to 250 nm for all doped-CD suspensions. This shoulder peak is attributed to the π–π* transitions within the sp2-hybridized graphitic core of CDs. The π–π* core band originates from conjugated π-orbitals of carbon rings (C=C) and is thus associated with the carbon core (crystalline), suggesting a large band gap that is associated with small sp2-hybridized carbon clusters embedded in an sp3-hybridized carbon matrix (Lin et al., 2022; Reckmeier et al., 2016). Based on this interpretation, Citric-180, which exhibits the most prominent shoulder peak, likely possesses a greater carbon cluster.

The existence of these distinct absorption peaks provides strong evidence for the successful creation of surface functionalized CDs via the hydrothermal treatment of the citric acid-cysteine mixture. Moreover, the optical properties of the suspensions obtained in this study align with those previously reported for intercalary semiconductor carbon nanomaterials (Liu et al., 2019). This suggests that the hydrothermal synthesis process utilized in this investigation successfully generates colloidal CDs.

Fig. 2a shows the photoluminescence (PL) emission characteristic of the as-obtained CDs aqueous under UV light exposure. The 10−2 diluted suspension exhibits a homogeneous bright violet-blue luminescence under UV light, highlighting the strong fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized CDs. Fig. 2b presents the PL emission spectrum of the CDs suspension upon excitation with fluorescent light. A prominent emission peak is observed at approximately 490, 496, and 503 nm. Notably, the peak positioned at 503 nm exhibits a blue shift towards 490 nm as the synthesis temperature increases from 160 °C to 200 °C. This shift suggests potential alterations in the surface state of the CDs due to varying synthesis conditions, as reported by Misra et al. (2018), aligning with observations from the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1a). Innocenzi and Stagi (2023) and Hola et al. (2017) attributed such blue shifts in both absorption and emission peaks to the position and number of N atoms on functionalized CDs.

Fig. 2c-e provide a more detailed analysis of the PL behavior of the synthesized CDs at various temperatures, focusing on their response to excitation. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 nm to 360 nm, a shift towards longer wavelengths (redshift) is detected in the emission peak of the CDs. As an illustration, Citric-160 demonstrates a more significant change in wavelength from 498 nm to 510 nm, while Citric-200 just reveals a shift from 489 nm to 490 nm. This observation indicates that the temperature at which the synthesis takes place influences the emission properties of the CDs. Significantly, CDs that are produced at lower temperatures show a more pronounced redshift when they are stimulated with longer wavelengths. In a study conducted by Innocenzi and Stagi (2023), it was found that graphitic-N sites can induce red-shifts. This is achieved by reducing the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) through the addition of extra electrons to the vacant π* orbitals of the conjugated system. Kundelev et al. (2019) additionally established a correlation between these phenomena and the existence of a greater concentration of amino groups on the CD surface. According to their findings, an augmentation in the quantity of amino groups results in a more significant displacement of the PL peak towards longer wavelengths (red-shift). According to current research, it may be deduced that CDs produced at lower temperatures are likely to have a higher concentration of surface N atoms and amino groups.

Fig. 3 displays representative TEM images of the CDs obtained at different synthesis temperatures. The images clearly show the influence of temperature on the size and morphology of the CDs. CD160 and CD200 exhibit a uniform distribution of particles with sizes smaller than 10 nm, indicating a well-controlled synthesis process. In contrast, CD180 exhibits a wider range of sizes, with particles varying in size from 10 nm to more than 100 nm. This indicates a more spontaneous development process at this temperature. The data are similar with the findings reported by Ludmerczki et al. (2019). They found that as the synthesis period increased, the particle size dispersion of citric acid-derived CDs became wider, ranging from 1 to 50 nm. This was especially evident in syntheses conducted at 180 °C for up to 120 min. Nevertheless, it is crucial to acknowledge that their research exclusively examined the temperature point of 180 °C, therefore restricting the capacity to make more extensive inferences regarding the impact of temperature across a broader spectrum.

The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis of samples from a 10−2 dilution provides further insights into the structural features of the CDs. The HRTEM images of CD160 and CD200 (Fig. 3a and c, respectively) reveal distinct 10 lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 3.3 nm and 2.0 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (100) lattice planes of graphite (Bhat et al., 2022; Tomskaya et al., 2022). These observations suggest that the CDs produced at these temperatures have a high degree of crystallinity. Conversely, the HRTEM image of CD180 (Fig. 3b) does not exhibit discernible lattice fringes. This observation indicates a lower degree of crystallinity or possibly an amorphous structure. According to Pal et al. (2020), the lack of distinct patterns in the fringes suggests that there is a greater fraction of sp3 carbon atoms in the CD structure, which leads to a less organized and more disordered nature. In agreement with this idea, Siddique et al. (2018) proposed that larger CDs frequently arise from amorphous clusters of carbon nanostructures that are evenly distributed and surrounded by oxygen and hydrogen atoms, especially when -CHO compounds are employed as precursors. The expansion of these shapeless carbon clusters is aided by the movement of additional molecules towards the surface of the particle. This procedure finally results in the creation of CDs that are self-passivated with functional groups including hydrogen and oxygen. This corresponds to the greater dimensions and broader dispersion exhibited in the current TEM image. In addition, EDS analysis revealed that C and O are the main elements present in the as-obtained CDs. Remarkably, S was detected across all synthesis temperatures, while N was not detectable using this technique.

The results of the inhibition zone assay (Fig. 4 ) demonstrate that the undiluted CD suspensions exhibit antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria. The CDs synthesized at 160 °C exhibited the highest activity, resulting in inhibition zones measuring 33.84 mm and 27.16 mm against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Conversely, CDs synthesized at 200 °C exhibited the least amount of antibacterial activity, with a diameter of 27.02 mm for S. aureus and 22.06 mm for E. coli. The CDs formed at 180 °C showed moderate activity (28.57 mm) against S. aureus, while their performance against E. coli (28.36 mm) was significantly decreased compared to the CDs synthesized at 160 °C.

Fig. 5 illustrates the inhibition zones of the undiluted CD suspensions against Penicillium spp. and Aspergillus niger after three days of incubation. On the Penicillium spp. plate, the control and blank samples exhibited no inhibition zone. Conversely, the fungal population surrounding the CD suspensions synthesized at 160, 180, and 200 °C displayed clear inhibition zones of 40.1 mm, 25.7 mm, and 23.5 mm, respectively. These findings indicate that the CD suspensions possess antifungal activity against Penicillium spp., with the CD200 suspension demonstrating the lowest inhibitory effect. Regarding Aspergillus niger, inhibition zones of 30.8 mm, 25.3 mm, and 23.7 mm were observed for CDs synthesized at 160, 180, and 200 °C, respectively. Notably, Aspergillus niger demonstrated greater resistance to the CDs compared to Penicillium spp., as evidenced by the smaller inhibition zones, particularly for CDs synthesized at 160 °C. Conversely, the CDs synthesized at 200 °C maintained comparable efficacy against both fungal species. Given the well-established fungicidal properties of sulfur due to its lipophilic nature (Roy Choudhury et al., 2011; Roy Choudhury et al., 2012), it is plausible that the sulfur content within the cysteine-doped CDs contributed significantly to their antifungal activity, particularly against Aspergillus niger. Furthermore, in accordance with previous research (Devecioglu et al., 2022), an increase in incubation period led to a decrease in antifungal activity. Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material corroborates this finding, demonstrating a significant decline in antifungal activity against both Penicillium spp. and Aspergillus niger on Day3 and Day5. The performance of the CD suspensions was thus influenced by time.

The observation from antibacterial and antifungal performance of CD from the different temperature condition suggests that particle size may not be the only factor that affects antimicrobial performance. The following study provides a detailed explanation and clarification of the factors that cause differences in activity at different synthesis temperatures.

Following synthesis, CD suspensions from each condition were incorporated into a PVOH matrix to form bionanocomposite films. These films were then subjected to further characterization to evaluate their potential applications in active packaging. Fig. 6 presents the FTIR spectra of a blank PVOH film compared to PVOH-CD films. As shown in Fig. 6a, the PVOH used in this study exhibited characteristics of partially hydrolyzed PVOH, with a strong peak at 1732 cm−1 corresponding to the presence of an acetate group (Soltani et al., 2007). The incorporation of CDs into the PVOH matrix resulted in the appearance of new peaks in the spectra of the PVOH-CD films (Fig. 6b and c). These new peaks, observed at 1595 and 644 cm−1, can be attributed to the presence of the functional groups on the CDs. The peak at 1595 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the COO− group from cysteine molecules functionalized on the CDs (Li et al., 2017; Si et al., 2015). Interestingly, its intensity is highest for CDs synthesized at 160 °C. This aligns with the known decomposition of amino acids between 190 °C and 240 °C (Yablokov et al., 2009), suggesting better cysteine preservation at 160 °C and its gradual decomposition at higher temperatures, reflected in the decreasing COO− peak intensity. Another new peak at 644 cm−1 (Fig. 6c) is attributed to the CS bond (Yang et al., 2020). Its highest intensity presents in the PVOH-CD180 film. It is well-known that cysteine is a precursor to carbon disulfide (CS2) (Banwart & Bremner, 1975; Gharehveran et al., 2020). During hydrothermal treatment, cysteine decomposition could generate thiol (–SH) and thiolate (R–S−) groups, potentially reacting with excited triplet states of dissolved organic matter to form CS2 (Gharehveran et al., 2020). In present study, the 160 °C temperature might be insufficient for thiol/thiolate generation, while 200 °C might be too high as Weiss et al. (2018) reported no CS2 detection upon complete cysteine decomposition. A minor signal corresponding to stretching vibrations of C–N= was seen at 1413 cm−1 (Fig. 6d), signifying the development of structures incorporating the doped N atoms (Yang et al., 2017), as the C–N= bond is absent in the original cysteine structure.

Beyond the emergence of new peaks, CD incorporation into the PVOH matrix led to broader bands and a more prominent peak in specific regions of the spectra. This broadening can be attributed to the overlapping of characteristic peaks from both PVOH and the doped-CDs (Fig. 6b). For instance, the peak at 1540–1555 cm−1 likely represents a combination of CN stretching vibrations and NH bending from amide II groups in bionanocomposite films (Barth & Haris, 2009; Sizeland et al., 2018). The peak at 1630 cm−1 can be ascribed to two possible origins. It could be due to the CN stretching vibration of imine groups potentially formed during the CD synthesis process (Suner et al., 2021). Alternatively, it might also correspond to vibration peaks of CC from citric acid-derived CDs (Lin et al., 2022). The broad peak at 1655–1670 cm−1 corresponds to CC stretching, potentially originating from the CDs and their intermediates (Ludmerczki et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020) since this band is absent in the blank PVOH film. Interestingly, the vibrational band at 667 cm−1 (Fig. 6c) presents a case of overlapping functionalities. It can be assigned to both the –OH wagging in PVOH (El Bahy et al., 2012; Smith, 2022; Zidan et al., 2019) and the stretching vibration of the benzene ring = C–H group from CD intermediates during the hydrothermal process of citric acid (Lin et al., 2022). This peak remained present in the spectrum of the blank PVOH and PVOH-CD180 film, while it disappeared in PVOH-CD160 and PVOH-CD200. Additionally, a pronounced peak at 1650 cm−1, attributed to “bound” water within the PVOH matrix, is observed in all samples (Jipa et al., 2012). As seen in Fig. 4e, the broad band observed in the range of 3500–3000 cm−1 encompasses OH or NH stretching vibrations (Suner et al., 2021). This band exhibited the highest intensity with pristine PVOH and decreased with CD incorporation. The lower intensity observed with higher synthesis temperature suggests an enhanced degree of dehydration/carbonization within the CDs (Yang et al., 2017). Conversely, the NH content appears to decrease with increasing temperature

FTIR analysis provided evidence for the formation of polyaromatic structures within the CDs. This is corroborated by the existence of a π–π* core band, which arises from the conjugated π-orbitals of carbon rings as detected in the UV–vis spectra. Furthermore, the FTIR analysis revealed the presence of various functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs, including –OH, CC, CN, NH, COO−, C–N= and CS. This observation aligns with the proposed mechanism of progressive L-cysteine polymerization onto citric acid intermediates during the hydrothermal process (Ludmerczki et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). Notably, the relative intensities of NH and COO− peaks were higher in the film prepared with CDs synthesized at 160 °C, indicating better preservation of cysteine functional groups. Conversely, the peak intensities attributed to CC and CS were highest for CDs synthesized at 180 °C. This suggests an optimal temperature for conserving certain intermediates from both citric acid and cysteine. These findings highlight a temperature-dependent influence on the incorporation and preservation of functional groups from the precursors.

CHNS analysis was employed to quantify the N and S content introduced via CD incorporation into the PVOH matrix (Table 1 ). As expected, the pristine PVOH film exhibited negligible N and S levels. The N content in the bionanocomposite films displayed a decreasing trend with increasing synthesis temperature, consistent with FTIR analysis observations. The PVOH-CD160 film exhibited the highest N content (0.21 wt%), while the film containing CD200 had the lowest (0.16 wt%). This finding correlates with the larger red-shift observed in the PL peak of CD160 (Fig. 2), potentially suggesting a link between N content and photoluminescence properties. Conversely, the S content followed a distinct pattern. The highest S content was observed in the PVOH-CD180 sample, which also aligns with the highest CS intensity in FTIR results. This observation correlates well with the trend observed in the antibacterial and antifungal performance data (Fig. 4 and Fig. 5), where the Citric-160 suspension exhibited the highest activity. These findings align with previous studies by Ezati et al. (2022), who reported that both N and S exhibit high antibacterial activities against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, with N-doped CDs demonstrating superior activity compared to S-doped CDs. However, S-doped CDs contributed significantly to fungicidal properties, particularly against Aspergillus niger (Roy Choudhury et al., 2011; Roy Choudhury et al., 2012). Therefore, based on the present findings, the N and S content within the CDs is likely the primary determinant of their antimicrobial and antifungal activity. Additionally, the results indicate that the bionanocomposite film embedded with CD180 possesses a lower carbon content.

The elemental composition and chemical structure of CDs were subsequently validated by XPS. The full-scan XPS spectra of all samples (Fig. 7 ) exhibited four distinct peaks, confirming the presence of C, O, N, and S elements. An extensive examination of the atomic fractions of these elements, as shown in Table S1 of the Supplementary Material, revealed a greater S fraction in CD180 than in CD160, supporting the results from FTIR and elemental analysis. However, the atomic fraction of N was below the detection limit in survey scans of CD180 and CD200, requiring high-resolution XPS analysis.

The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of PVOH-CD160 was deconvoluted into four distinct peaks at 284.98, 286.42, 288.01, and 289.16 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N/C–S, CN, and COO− functional groups, respectively (Wang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). The dominant peak at approximately 284.92 eV, assigned to sp2 or sp3 hybridized CC or CH bonds, represents the aliphatic carbon backbone of PVOH and potentially cysteine. This peak exhibited an increase in intensity with increasing synthesis temperature. Conversely, the intensity of peaks at around 286.42 eV (C–O/C–N/C–S) and 289.16 eV (COO−) decreased with increasing synthesis temperature. The reduction in peak intensity can be attributed to the gradual thermal decomposition of the cysteine moiety at higher temperatures, consistent with the FTIR results. The O1s spectrum of PVOH-CD160 was deconvoluted into four peaks at 531.34, 532.46, 533.19, and 534.23 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, C–O–C and -OH groups, respectively, likely originating from physiosorbed water or surface hydroxyls resulting from absorbed moisture.

The high-resolution N1s spectrum of PVOH-CD160 exhibited two peaks. The peak at 400.07 eV is attributed to pyrrolic/pyridinic-N, characterized by the presence of aromatic C=N–C bonds, while the peak at 401.04 eV corresponds to quaternary or graphitic-N, associated with tertiary N–(C)3 bonds (Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023). Notably, at elevated synthesis temperatures (180 and 200 °C), the N1s spectrum displayed a single predominant peak assigned to graphitic-N. This observation is supported by the absence of the CN peak (around 288.01 eV) in PVOH-CD180 and PVOH-CD200. Pillar-Little and Kim (2017) reported that at hydrothermal temperatures exceeding 150 °C, the relative proportion of pyrrolic/pyridinic-N decreases, transforming into graphitic-N, which exhibits greater thermal stability. Furthermore, high-resolution S2p spectra exhibited two peaks at approximately 163.78 and 165.00 eV, attributed to S2p3/2 of COSH and S2p1/2 of the CS covalent bonds in thiophene/thiazine (Wang et al., 2020), hence corroborating the findings on S compounds in FTIR.

The incorporation of CDs into the PVOH matrix resulted in a significant increase in the contact angle of the bionanocomposite films as reported in Table 2 . This increase appeared independent of the synthesis temperature. According to established principles (Krainer & Hirn, 2021; Kwok & Neumann, 1999), contact angle is typically governed by a combination of factors, including surface roughness and polarity. To investigate the influence of surface morphology, Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material presents SEM images of the pristine PVOH film and the bionanocomposite films. Notably, the addition of CDs appears to have a minimal effect on the surface morphology when compared to the pristine PVOH film. Both pristine and bionanocomposite films exhibit a similar degree of surface smoothness. Therefore, the observed increase in contact angle can be attributed primarily to an enhancement in hydrophobicity arising from the presence of the CDs within the PVOH matrix. This hypothesis is further supported by the decrease in the intensity of the OH stretching vibration peak in the FTIR spectra, observed in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1. This decrease suggests a reduction in the overall hydroxyl content within the bionanocomposite films compared to the pristine PVOH film.

The colorimetric measurements, using the CIE system (Table 2), showed a significant change in the appearance of PVOH films after including CD. The bionanocomposite films exhibited an elevation in the b* value and a slight decrease in the a* value compared to the pristine PVOH film, suggesting a yellowish tint with a green hue. The PVOH-CD180 film had the most prominent yellowish, as evidenced by its highest b* value.

Fig. 8a compares the optical characteristics of pure PVOH and PVOH-CD films under UV light illumination against a black background. As expected, the pristine PVOH film appears transparent under white light (Fig. 8b) and exhibits no fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 8a). Conversely, the bionanocomposite films, while remaining transparent in the visible region (Fig. 8b), display strong fluorescence upon UV light exposure (Fig. 8a). Notably, the PVOH-CD180 film exhibits the most intense fluorescence, significantly brightening the black background. This observation aligns well with the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 8b), where the PVOH-CD180 film demonstrates the highest absorbance. This correlation suggests that amorphous characteristics might influence the observed fluorescence intensity. While previous studies have reported similar photoluminescence characteristics for both crystalline and amorphous CDs (Siddique et al., 2018), the present findings suggest that particle size differences between crystalline and amorphous structures might affect defect/trap-related emissive centers and surface states, which play a dominant role in light emission from CDs. This could explain the observed variation in emission brightness. Interestingly, the UV–vis spectra also reveal a slight blue-shift in the absorption peak with increasing synthesis temperature. This shift is a characteristic feature of CD suspension and is preserved in the spectra of the bionanocomposite films, as seen in Fig. 1. Fig. 8c demonstrates the UV-blocking performance of the films. While the pristine PVOH film allows UV light transmission due to its low UV absorption, all the bionanocomposite films exhibit strong UV-blocking capabilities, particularly in the UVC and UVA range.

Citric acid-derived CDs have been reported to exhibit broad-spectrum antioxidant properties due to the abundance of reductive functional groups (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023; Wang et al., 2020). Similarly, N and S-containing compounds like cysteine have demonstrated antioxidant capacity comparable to well-established phenolic compounds (Romanet et al., 2019). As shown in Table 3 , the incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the antioxidant activity of the PVOH film. This improved antioxidant performance can be attributed to the presence of various functional groups on the CD surface, as determined by FTIR and XPS analysis. Notably, both PVOH-CD160 and PVOH-CD180 exhibited similar antioxidant performance, achieving approximately 80% DPPH radical scavenging activity, while, the PVOH-CD200 film displayed the highest performance, reaching around 89% scavenging activity. This enhancement in antioxidant performance is complex and could be attributed to several factors. Innocenzi and Stagi (2023) described the oxidant-antioxidant crossover capability of CDs, suggesting that their performance could be influenced by either electron-donating (–NH2, –OH, –OR’, –OCOR’) or electron-withdrawing (–COOH, –CH, NH3 +, NO2) groups, as well as defects on the CD surface arising from sp3-like defects or free edges. Therefore, it is challenging to definitively identify the specific dominant mechanism responsible for the antioxidant activity. Further investigation is required to elucidate the precise mechanism underlying this enhanced antioxidant behavior.

The antimicrobial efficacy of the bionanocomposite films against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria was assessed (Table 3 and Table S2 in Supplementary Material). Initially, films were made using a 3 wt% CD loading in PVOH. A polymer suspension was diluted by 9:1 ratio for the purpose of fabricating a film. The PVOH films contained CD160, CD180, and CD200 had exceptional antibacterial efficacy against Gram-positive bacteria, resulting in a reduction of more than 99.999%. Nevertheless, only the PVOH-CD160 and PVOH-CD180 films shown comparable effectiveness in combating Gram-negative bacteria, achieving a reduction of over 99.999% (5 log10 CFU/mL reduction). The PVOH-CD200 film exhibited a 99.99% reduction (4 log10 CFU/mL reduction) in performance when exposed to Gram-negative bacteria. To examine the impact of CD loading, a lower concentration (0.3 wt%) was utilized. The films were fabricated using a polymer suspension ratio of 99:1. The films exhibited antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria, resulting in a reduction of 95% - 97% (1 log10 CFU/mL reduction). Nevertheless, their ability to effectively combat Gram-negative bacteria reduced dramatically, falling below 20%. This difference in susceptibility can be attributed to the distinct cell wall structures of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria serves as a significant barrier to various antibiotics, where Gram-positive bacteria lack this protective layer. As reported by Breijyeh et al. (2020), this structural difference renders Gram-negative bacteria inherently more resistant to antibiotics compared to Gram-positive counterparts. Similar observations have been made regarding antimicrobial peptides, where complete inhibition of Gram-positive S. aureus was achieved within 30 min, while eradication of Gram-negative E. coli required 3 h, likely due to the presence of the outer membrane (Nam et al., 2018). The excellent antimicrobial performance of the PVOH-CD films is likely attributed to the presence of various functional groups on the CD surface, as identified through FTIR and XPS analysis. These functional groups are postulated to generate reactive oxygen species, inducing significant DNA damage or oxidative stress in microorganisms (Suner et al., 2021). Additionally, the presence of N and S on the surface has been associated with antimicrobial properties (Ezati et al., 2022; Sahiner et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as active packaging for sweet bread
ce:section.ce:para: Sweet bread was chosen as a food system to assess the efficacy of active packaging derived from PVOH-CD film, as sweet bread is particularly susceptible to bacteria and mold, commonly present in the environment, leading to spoilage during its shelf life. Aw is a critical parameter for predicting microbial growth. While white bread typically exhibits an Aw of 0.95–0.97, Aspergillus species proliferate at Aw values exceeding 0.80–0.87, and bacterial growth commences above 0.87–0.97 (Magan & Aldred, 2006; Tapia et al., 2020). Table 4 reports the changes in Aw and hardness of bread crumb during storage. The initial Aw measurement of the sweet bread used in this study (0.88) indicated a high risk of both fungal and bacterial spoilage.

A decrease in Aw was observed over time for all samples, including the control, although this reduction was more pronounced in bread slices packaged with PVOH film. This phenomenon is likely attributed to the hygroscopic nature of the PVOH film, which may have absorbed moisture from the bread. Visual inspection of the bread (Fig. 9 ) revealed the onset of mold growth on both the control and PVOH-packaged samples by Day4, whereas the bread packaged with PVOH-CD remained mold-free until Day6. Concurrently, the Aw of the bread tended to decrease while the bread crumb hardness increased over the storage period, even in the control sample. The lowest Aw (0.806) was recorded for bread slices packaged with PVOH-CD160 film on Day6. Notably, this Aw value remains within the growth range for Aspergillus niger, which requires a minimum Aw of 0.77 (Parra et al., 2004). While the reduction in Aw and the increase in hardness might contribute to a reduced likelihood of mold growth, the results indicate that these factors alone are insufficient to prevent mold contamination. Despite the moisture transfer to the PVOH film, the bread packaged with this film still developed mold. In contrast, the bread packaged with PVOH-CD remained mold-free, suggesting that the CDs played a more significant role in inhibiting mold growth than the moisture reduction within the bread. These findings align with the excellent antifungal activity of CDs reported in Fig. 5.

This study successfully synthesized CDs from citric acid via a hydrothermal process. The results revealed that the S, N-doping agent and synthesis temperature significantly influenced the properties and characteristics of the CDs. Undoped CDs exhibited weak signals in the UV–vis spectrum, indicating minimal presence of characteristic CD features. Conversely, all S, N-doped CDs displayed typical CD signatures in the UV–vis spectra, including n−π* and π–π* transitions, and exhibited strong photoluminescence under 365 nm light. The synthesis temperature (160, 180, and 200 °C) had a pronounced effect on the characterization and functionalities of both the CD suspensions and the resulting bionanocomposite films. TEM analysis revealed that CDs obtained at 160 °C and 200 °C were crystalline with dimensions below 10 nm. In contrast, CDs synthesized at 180 °C displayed an amorphous cluster with dimensions ranging from 10 to 100 nm, lacking well-defined lattice fringes. Lower synthesis temperatures (160 °C) facilitated better preservation of the cysteine dopant, leading to high S and N content as well as a stronger presence of –COO functional groups. The CD suspensions demonstrated significant antimicrobial activity against S. aureus, E. coli, Penicillium spp., and Aspergillus niger, as evidenced by large clear zones. The lowest activity was observed for CDs synthesized at 200 °C. This antimicrobial activity was maintained after incorporating the CD suspensions into bionanocomposite films with PVOH. All PVOH-CD films exhibited a 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-positive bacteria. However, only PVOH-CD160 and PVOH-CD180 films achieved a 5 log10 CFU/mL reduction in Gram-negative bacteria, while PVOH-CD200 films showed only a 4 log10 CFU/mL reduction under contact conditions at 37 °C for 24 h. This difference likely stems from the lower S and N content observed in CDs synthesized at 200 °C. In contrast, the antioxidant activity of the PVOH film improved to over 80% DPPH scavenging after incorporating CDs. The PVOH-CD200 bionanocomposite film exhibited the highest performance at 88.95% scavenging. Studies on the shelf-life of sweet bread suggest that the PVOH-CD films could extend the product's shelf-life regardless of the CD type. This is attributed to the reactive species and the presence of S and N doping on the CD surface.

Pornchanok Thanawutthiphong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Thitiporn Kaewpetch: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Wanwitoo Wanmolee: Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Seonghyuk Ko: Validation, Supervision. Nattinee Bumbudsanpharoke: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
